system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Client Business Intelligence

Systematic approach to researching a client business, identifying opportunities, and preparing a pitch.

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Surface-Level Research (30 min)

### Source Priority (run in this order, stop when you have enough)

1. **Their current website** — identify all pages, products, about page, press page, contact. Check for `/nosotros/`, `/tiendas/`, `/prensa/`, `/blog/` — these often have buried gold (subsidiaries, partnerships, director names).
2. **Infonegocios / ABC Color / Última Hora** — PY business press. Use curl + sed for article extraction since these sites are usually accessible without JS. Search: `"{company}" director gerente dueño`
3. **LinkedIn** — `/company/{name}` for corporate page. Note: ALWAYS blocked by auth wall. Can confirm page EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry.
4. **Google Maps** — `https://www.google.com/maps/search/{company}+Paraguay/`. Use the browser tool, then extract `[role=feed]` entries for store names, addresses, ratings, phones.
5. **Facebook** — `/pages/{name}` or `/{name}`. Usually accessible, basic info visible.
6. **DuckDuckGo lite** — `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={query}'` — no-JS, no-block, good for PY corporate queries
7. **Bing** — fallback when Google blocks. `/search?q={query}` with a desktop UA usually works.
8. **Corporate registry** — SET RUC (`dnit.gov.py/web/portal-institucional/consulta-ruc?ruc={RUC}`), Mercantil Registry, InfoCiudad. WARNING: These almost always return 404. Try once then move on.

### Search Queries to Try

```bash
# Ownership/directors
"{company} dueños directores familia"
"{company} gerente director"
"{company} nombre del dueño"

# Subsidiaries
"{company} Argentina filial"
"{company} subsidiaria"

# Press articles
"\"{company}\" Infonegocios"
"\"{company}\" ABC Color"
"\"{company}\" Última Hora"

# General when others fail
curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={company}+Paraguay'
```

**For web scraping without getting blocked:**
- Use `curl -sL 'https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...' -H 'User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0'` for search results
- Use `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q=...'` for simple text extraction
- For blocked sites: Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora are good sources for PY corporate news
- LinkedIn auth wall always blocks — note that the profile EXISTS but can't read without login
- Google blocks aggressive IPs — use Bing or DDG as fallback

### Phase 2: Deep Research (1-2 hours)

1. **Read press articles** — search Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora for interviews with directors
2. **Extract key quotes** — director strategy quotes are gold for understanding priorities
3. **Map corporate structure** — parent, subsidiaries, strategic partners, major clients
4. **Product family analysis** — every product line, cross-sell pairs, pricing tiers, warranty differences
5. **Competitive landscape** — list direct competitors, their focus, Superspuma's advantages

### Phase 3: Opportunity Analysis

1. **Cross-sell math** — calculate the upsell potential per customer (e.g., mattress + base + protector + almohada = +Gs. 1.17M)
2. **B2B opportunities** — wholesale client portals, hotel/hospitality programs, government contracts
3. **Market gaps** — what competitors don't do that this client could
4. **Geographic expansion** — Argentina subsidiary (if exists), Brazil border cities, Mercosur advantages

### Phase 4: Contact Documentation

1. **Research the contact** — LinkedIn, news mentions, Google
2. **Document what's known vs unknown** — be honest about gaps
3. **Map authority level** — can they make decisions? Who else needs to be involved?
4. **Build relationship strategy** — warm intro? Cold outreach? Existing relationship?

### Phase 5: Pitch Preparation

Create `contacts/deal-strategy.md` with:
- Deal options (tiered from low to high scope)
- Step-by-step conversation flow (open with value → show → ask → present opportunity → close)
- Talking points (what to highlight, what NOT to say)
- Objection handling for likely concerns
- Competitive positioning (why us vs agencies)
- Pricing signals (monthly retainer, project-based, revenue share)

## File Structure for Client Documentation

```
{client-repo}/
├── contacts/
│   ├── README.md              # Index of all contacts & documents
│   ├── {contact-name}.md       # Profile + relationship strategy
│   └── deal-strategy.md        # Pitch guide + deal options
├── docs/
│   ├── business-analysis.md    # Corporate research
│   └── opportunity-analysis.md # Deal opportunities
```

### Service-at-Home Safety Protocol

When the client offers a **mobile/at-home service** (depilación a domicilio, delivery, etc.), proactively build a safety protocol addressing:

**Risk:** "Alguien pide, voy y me roban."

**Mitigation layers:**
1. **Pre-verification (call, not just text)** — Llmar antes de ir. Si no contesta, no vas.
2. **Location sharing** — Compartir ubicación en vivo con contacto de confianza.
3. **Check-in/out protocol** — "Voy a [dirección]" → "Entré" → "Salí bien". Timer de 30 min.
4. **Intuition rule** — Si algo se siente mal al llegar, cancelás. Decís "perdón, me surgió un problema".
5. **First-session filter** — Ofrecer primera sesión gratis/descuento profundo a las primeras X personas. Sirve como filtro y genera confianza mutua.
6. **No carry cash** — Preferir transferencia bancaria. Menos incentivo al robo.

Documentar en `docs/safety-protocol.md` en el repo del cliente. Incluir checklist diario imprimible y plan de emergencia (qué hacer si pasa algo). Ver `references/service-at-home-safety.md` para template completo.

---

### Phase 6: Service Expansion Research (new service line for existing client)

When a client with an existing business wants to add a NEW service line targeting an underserved segment (e.g., affirmative depilation for trans women, men's grooming, post-op care):

**Step 1: 20-Question Structured Questionnaire with Multiple-Choice Options**

Format: Markdown with 4 blocks of 5 questions each (20 total). Each question has 4-5 pre-generated options (a/b/c/d/e). The MC format forces the client to decide fast instead of rambling.

**Important: include blank "Otro: ________" lines** — clients often combine or modify options. Document their exact words, not the closest option.

Blocks are always:
- Block 1 (Q1-4): Validación personal / Motivación
- Block 2 (Q5-8): Equipo / Técnica
- Block 3 (Q9-12): Precios / Modelo de negocio
- Block 4 (Q13-16): Marketing / Contacto comunitario
- Block 5 (Q17-20): Seguridad / Privacidad / Riesgos

After the client responds, **make the remaining decisions as IA** — don't send the parsed answers back for approval. The client said "decide todo como IA". This includes: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy variations. Document the reasoning for each decision.

If the client leaves a question open ("investiga online y decide"), research and decide. Don't ask again.

2. **Análisis de respuestas** — Las respuestas del dueño revelan prioridades, presupuesto, conocimientos, y miedos. Documentar textualmente, incluyendo correcciones y anotaciones que el dueño agrega a las opciones pre-generadas. Extract all "decide todo como IA" signals and act on them.

3. **Research en 7 lotes paralelos** — Dividir los 100+ temas en lotes: A) Mercado/competencia, B) Máquina/equipo, C) Comunidad, D) Médico, E) Marketing, F) Finanzas, G) Psicología, H) Logística. Investigar cada lote y compilar en un documento master.

4. **Competitor pricing via browser scraping** — Para clínicas/competidores PY:
   - Abrir con `browser_navigate` los sitios de competidores directos
   - Usar `browser_snapshot(full=true)` para capturar precios, zonas, packs
   - Extraer precios reales visibles en el HTML (ej: DepilArte tenía Gs. 49,000-220,000 visibles)
   - Si el sitio está bloqueado, probar `curl -sL` con User-Agent desktop o DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...`)
   - Para sitios sin precios visibles, estimar basado en: tamaño de zona (mini/pink/pequeña/mediana/especial/grande), y precio promedio de mercado PY

5. **Search engine evasion** — Google, Bing, Brave, and Ecosia all block VPS IPs after 1-2 requests. DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`html.duckduckgo.com/html`) works but also blocks after ~3 requests with a CAPTCHA. Recovery strategies:
   - Send requests with rotated User-Agent headers via `curl`
   - Use Browserbase (when available) which routes through residential proxies
   - Fall back to training data knowledge for common business intelligence topics (Paraguay market, IPL pricing, trans community orgs)
   - Accept blocked results as "not accessible via automation" rather than retrying endlessly

6. **Master document compilation** — Después de investigar todos los lotes, compilar en `docs/trans-research-master.md` (o nombre análogo) con:
   - Cada lote como sección A-H
   - Una tabla de resumen ejecutivo al final con decisiones clave (precio, descuento, marketing, plan)
   - Subir a repo y dar link al dueño

7. **100-item research checklist** — After the master document, generate a separate file `docs/{topic}-research-100-list.md` with 100 specific questions/items organized in the same categories. This serves as: progress tracker, idea source for next iterations, and documentation of research scope. Each item is 1 sentence.

8. **Website audit for existing clients** — When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:
   - **Structure map** — every page, component, and content file
   - **Content audit** — es.json (or equivalent): find placeholders ("****"), duplicates (two FAQ sections), missing sections (no mention of new service)
   - **UX/Design** — photos vs emoji, social proof, about section, favicon
   - **SEO** — sitemap, robots, og:image, meta tags, schema
   - **Performance** — DOM size, load time, cache headers
   - **Score each category** 1-10 with justification
   - **Prioritized improvement plan** — 3 tiers: 🔴 esta semana, 🟡 este mes, 🟢 cuando haya tiempo. Each item has: effort estimate, impact assessment, specific files to change.

9. **Action plan from audit** — After the audit:
   - Decide all pricing/package decisions as IA
   - Generate a 7-day checklist (día por día) 
   - Generate a 30-day plan with milestones
   - Generate WhatsApp messages ready to send (4 templates: new client, confirmation, 24h follow-up, 20-day reminder)
   - Push all docs to repo, give client the GitHub link

## Pitfalls

- **LinkedIn always requires login** — accept that you'll only see the auth wall. Note the profile EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry multiple times.
- **Instagram blocks all automated access** — `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE` always. Don't waste time retrying Instagram directly. See `references/instagram-osint.md` for workarounds.
- **Google blocks aggressively** — after 2-3 searches from the same IP, you get a CAPTCHA wall. Switch to DDG lite or Bing immediately.
- **PY corporate registries are unreliable** — SET (DNIT), Mercantil, InfoCiudad often return 404. News articles are more reliable than official registries. Always try once but don't spend more than 30 seconds.
- **Paraguay business owners often have low digital footprint** — many executives have no LinkedIn, no social media. "No public results found" is a valid finding, not a failed search.
- **Warm leads are better than cold research** — if the user has a direct contact (like Leticia Roig as GM of Superspuma), prioritize documenting what they know over searching publicly.
- **Don't rely on browser for everything** — Curl with simple User-Agent headers often works where the headless browser gets blocked. Try `curl -sL` before opening the browser for each source.
- **Infonegocios articles are gold** — they often have director interviews with strategy quotes, company ambitions, competitive claims. Extract the full article text, not just snippets.
- **See `references/search-engine-evasion-pattern.md`** for the full ordered fallback chain when Google/DDG/Bing/Brave all block automated queries. Yahoo is the most reliable VPS-search-engine as of 2026-05. Wikipedia API always works. Instagram data can be extracted from Yahoo search result snippets.

### Pitfall: Template/boilerplate leftovers

When the client site was generated from a multi-tenant builder like `paragu-ai-builder`, check for **stale template files** in `lib/client-kit/` (or equivalent). These are the generic versions of the actual components in `components/`. They are NOT used by the live site but confuse future edits. Recommend cleaning them up in a follow-up commit.

**Detection:** `find . -not -path './node_modules/*' -not -path './.git/*' -not -path './.next/*' -type f | sort` shows component files in TWO locations (e.g. `components/header.tsx` + `lib/client-kit/header.tsx`). The `components/` versions are the live ones.

## Website Audit (for existing clients)

When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:

### Structure map
- Every page, component, and content file. Output: tree of `app/`, `components/`, `content/`.

### Content audit
- Check `es.json` (or equivalent): find **placeholders** (`"****"`, `"placeholder": true`), **duplicates** (two FAQ arrays), **missing sections** (no mention of new service line), **stale references**.
- Check every page — does it load? Does it have content?

### UX/Design scoring
- Score each category 1-10: Funcionalidad, Diseño, Contenido, Prueba Social, SEO, Performance, UX Móvil
- Justify each score in 1 sentence
- Identify the biggest gap (typically "Contenido" or "Prueba Social")

### SEO checklist
- Favicon? (link rel="icon" in head)
- og:image for social shares?
- Sitemap.xml? robots.txt?
- Canonical URL?
- JSON-LD schema?

### Performance
- Load time (from browser performance API)
- DOM size
- Cache headers
- HTTP protocol (h3 is good)

### Prioritized improvement plan
3 tiers with effort estimate and impact:
- 🔴 **Critical** (hacer esta semana) — e.g., favicon, placeholder fix, new service section, Instagram link, photo of owner
- 🟡 **Medium** (hacer este mes) — e.g., real photos, og:image, unified FAQs, social proof counters
- 🟢 **Low** (cuando haya tiempo) — e.g., blog, dark mode, animations, chatbot

Each item should specify the file(s) to change and the exact line/region.

### Implementation after audit

When the client says "implementa todos" or "decide todo como IA":
1. Apply ALL changes identified in the audit
2. For pricing/service decisions left open by the client: research, decide, document reasoning
3. Build: `npm run build` (or equivalent) and fix any errors
4. Git commit + push
5. Docker build + deploy
6. If deploy fails (resources), note it and tell the client what manual step is needed

### Visual Artist Research

When researching a visual artist (muralist, painter, illustrator) for a website project, use `references/artist-visual-research-pattern.md` for the full ordered workflow. Key differences from business research:

- **No corporate registry** — no RUC, no SET. Use Wikidata + Wikipedia API + news sites.
- **Press articles are the richest source** — interviews reveal style, influences, quotes, career timeline.
- **YouTube is underutilized** — extraction of `ytInitialData` from HTML gives video interviews that news articles don't cover.
- **Name confusion is common** — verify with 2+ sources before concluding. Paraguayan artists may have 1-2 press articles but no Wikipedia presence.
- **Website plan is the deliverable** — unlike business research (deal strategy), artist research produces a complete website architecture (10 pages minimum) with content specification per page, tech stack, monetization strategy, and roadmap.

## Per-session reference file management

After each major research session for a client:
1. Create `references/{client}-{topic}-{date}.md` with condensed findings
2. This file is the **session transcript summary** — not a substitute for the full docs in the client repo
3. Keep it focused on: key findings, techniques learned, decisions that future sessions should know
4. Delete the oldest reference when the list gets too long (5+ files) — the repo docs are the source of truth

## `decide-todo-como-IA` pattern

When the client responds to the 20-question questionnaire and leaves decisions open with "decide todo como IA" or "investiga online y decide":
- Research each open question (competitor pricing, technical specs, community contacts)
- Take a firm decision — don't propose options back
- Document the reasoning for each decision in markdown
- Include a table: "Decisiones de la IA basadas en investigación"
- This applies to: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy

## Verification

After creating the documentation files:
1. Push to repo: `git add contacts/ docs/ && git commit && git push`
2. Verify the site content reflects the research (update es.json with new company info)
3. If adding stores with GPS, add Google Maps links to the store locator page
4. Cross-sell opportunities should be reflected in product page design (show bundles)

name: brand-positioning-from-repos
description: Extract brand positioning, audience definition, customer pains, and landing page copy from a company's actual project repos — not theory. Use when a founder says "help me with positioning" or needs website copy grounded in reality.
version: 1.0.0

    tags: [positioning, branding, copywriting, landing-page, audience, strategy]

# Brand Positioning from Repo Analysis

Derive positioning, audience, pains, and conversion copy from what a company has actually built — not aspirational claims.

## When to Use

- Founder asks for help with positioning, messaging, or landing page copy
- Company website doesn't match what the company actually does
- Need to tighten audience definition with evidence
- Writing or rewriting a homepage/landing page

## Step 1: Repo Audit for Audience Extraction

Pull all repos with metadata:
```bash
gh repo list ORG --limit 50 --json name,description,primaryLanguage,repositoryTopics,isPrivate,pushedAt | cat
```

Categorize every repo by CLIENT TYPE (not tech stack):
- Healthcare, Retail, Energy, Automotive, Education, etc.
- Note which are client projects vs internal tools vs research

### Key Questions to Answer
- Who is ACTUALLY paying? (client projects reveal real audience)
- What industries repeat? (pattern = sweet spot)
- What's the company size of clients? (SMB vs enterprise)
- What geography? (but don't limit to geography unless founder wants to)

## Step 2: Positioning Sentence

Format: "We help [specific audience] achieve [specific outcome] using [unique mechanism]"

### Rules
- Audience = defined by SITUATION not demographics or geography
- Outcome = what the client gets, not what you do
- Mechanism = what makes you different from alternatives
- Never mention tool names (no "using Claude and Cursor")
- Provide 3-4 options, recommend one, let founder choose direction

### Common Trap
Founder says "our audience is anyone who needs X" — repos always tell a tighter story. Present the evidence, let them decide whether to broaden.

## Step 3: Pain Extraction

Derive 3-5 pains from the TYPE of projects built:
- If clients had no tech team → "We don't have developers and can't afford to hire"
- If projects replaced manual workflows → "Everything is still manual"
- If clients tried generic tools first → "We tried ChatGPT but couldn't make it work for our business"
- If projects were rebuilds/rescues → "We paid for software before and it never got finished"
- If training was part of delivery → "We don't know where to start with AI"

Each pain must map to something the company actually solved in a real project.

## Step 4: Current Website Gap Analysis

Extract current site content and compare against repo reality:
```
web_extract(["https://company-site.com"])
```

Check for:
1. Hero talks about tools instead of outcomes → rewrite
2. No problem/pain section → visitor has no "that's me" moment
3. Testimonials look fake (generic names, no photos) → hurts more than helps, remove or replace with real case studies
4. Geography limiting ("serving X region") when skills are market-agnostic → drop unless strategic
5. Mixed languages in copy → looks unfinished
6. Internal goals stated as client benefits ("our vision is to be the leader in X") → remove
7. Generic service descriptions that match every competitor → differentiate

## Step 5: Landing Page Content Template

Fill this structure — every section pushes toward CTA:

### 1. HERO (above the fold)
- Headline: Main outcome in simple terms
- Subheadline: Who you help + problem + what you do differently
- CTA: Primary action (Book a call)
- Trust line: Quick credibility from real projects

### 2. PROBLEM (recognition)
- "Does this sound familiar?"
- 3-5 pain bullets from Step 3

### 3. SOLUTION (your approach)
- Reframing idea (what you do differently)
- 2-3 line explanation
- 3 key differentiators

### 4. OUTCOMES (results)
- 4-6 concrete results clients get
- Use language from actual project deliverables

### 5. TRUST (proof)
- Mini cases from REAL repos (anonymize if needed but keep industry)
- Industries served (extracted from repo categories)
- Only use testimonials if they're real

### 6. PROCESS (reduce complexity)
- 3-4 steps max
- Understand → Design → Build → Deliver+Train

### 7. FINAL CTA
- Reinforce outcome
- Remove fear/friction
- Same CTA as hero

## Rules
- No paragraph longer than 2-3 lines
- No jargon
- If it doesn't help conversion → remove it
- Every claim must trace back to a real project
- Don't use fake testimonial names — use "Verified Client" or real ones

## Pitfalls

1. **Don't let founder's aspiration override evidence** — "we serve everyone" is never true. Show the repo data.
2. **Geography is where you've been, not where you have to stay** — unless founder wants geographic focus, define audience by situation not location.
3. **Tool names in hero copy kill conversion** — nobody outside tech knows what Cursor or [REMOVED] is.
4. **Fake testimonials hurt more than no testimonials** — generic names + generic titles + no photos = instant credibility loss.
5. **Internal vision ≠ client benefit** — "position Paraguay as AI leader" means nothing to a prospect.

for r in repos:

    topics = r.get('repositoryTopics') or []
    desc = r['description'] or '(no description)'
    print(f\"{r['name']:35s} | {lang:12s} | {desc:.70s}\")
" | cat", "timeout": 15}

{"code": "from hermes_tools import read_file, write_file

content = read_file("/tmp/nico-portfolio/app/page.tsx")["content"]

# Replace all old purple references with new deeper mystic
replacements = [
    ("rgba(139,92,246,0.4)", "rgba(142,26,255,0.4)"),
    ("bg-mystic-950/60", "bg-mystic-950/60"),
    ("border-mystic-700/50", "border-mystic-700/50"),
    ("text-mystic-300", "text-mystic-300"),
    ("text-mystic-400", "text-mystic-400"),
    ("text-mystic-500", "text-mystic-500"),
    ("border-mystic-500/50", "border-mystic-500/50"),
    ("bg-mystic-900/30", "bg-mystic-900/30"),
    ("from-purple-500 to-violet-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-700"),
    ("from-violet-500 to-indigo-600", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-800"),
    ("from-fuchsia-500 to-purple-600", "from-mystic-400 to-mystic-700"),
    ("from-purple-500 to-pink-600", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-600"),
    ("from-indigo-500 to-purple-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-500"),
    ("from-violet-500 to-fuchsia-600", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-400"),
    ("from-purple-600 to-violet-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-500"),
    ("from-purple-500 to-violet-600", "from-mystic-600 to-mystic-700"),
    ("bg-mystic-800/50", "bg-mystic-800/50"),
    ("border-mystic-700/40", "border-mystic-700/40"),
    ("bg-mystic-500", "bg-mystic-500"),
    ("from-mystic-500 to-mystic-400", "from-mystic-500 to-mystic-400"),
    ("text-mystic-500", "bg-mystic-500"),
    ("text-mystic-500", "text-mystic-500"),
    ("border-mystic-800/30", "border-mystic-800/30"),
]

# Remove redundant from-purple-600 that doesn't match
# Let me just check what patterns exist
import re
purple_refs = re.findall(r'(from-[\w-]+|to-[\w-]+|bg-[\w/-]+|text-[\w-]+|border-[\w/-]+|rgba\([^)]+\))', content)
unique = set(purple_refs)
print("Found color refs:", sorted(unique))

# Check for remaining old purple after our replacements
old_purple = [r for r in unique if 'purple' in r or 'violet' in r or 'fuchsia' in r or 'indigo' in r or 'pink' in r]
print("\nOld tailwind colors remaining:", old_purple)
"}

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "client-content-audit", "description": "Audit a client repo's content against a questionnaire/source-of-truth — find gaps, fix them, and surface what the client still needs to provide. Covers questionnaire ingestion, gap analysis, source-of-truth generation, and content implementation.", "tags": ["client", "content", "audit", "questionnaire", "gap-analysis", "paraguai", "dayah", "el-viajero"], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: client-content-audit
description: "Audit a client repo's content against a questionnaire/source-of-truth — find gaps, fix them, and surface what the client still needs to provide. Covers questionnaire ingestion, gap analysis, source-of-truth generation, and content implementation."
tags: [client, content, audit, questionnaire, gap-analysis, paraguai, dayah, el-viajero]
---

# Client Content Audit & Questionnaire Implementation

Audit a client website repo against their filled questionnaire / source-of-truth. Find every gap, fix what can be fixed with data already available, and document what requires client action.

## When to Use

- Client provided a questionnaire with answers and you need to verify implementation
- Taking over a partially-built client site and need to assess completeness
- Before client relaunch — ensure all content promises are delivered
- After a conversation with the client produced new data that needs to be incorporated

## Process

### 1. Ingest the Questionnaire

The questionnaire may be in `docs/` as `questionnaire.md`, `source-of-truth.md`, or `client-questionnaire.md`. Three tiers of detail:

- **`client-questionnaire.md`** — blank template (2KB, section headings only, no answers)
- **`questionnaire.md`** — filled form with client's answers (15-20KB)
- **`source-of-truth.md`** — structured canonical reference (10-15KB)

Read all three. The `source-of-truth.md` is the most authoritative — if it exists, use it as primary.

### 2. Map Questionnaire -> Content Inventory

Check every section of the questionnaire against the app's content:

```python
import json

# Read content
content = json.load(open("content/es.json"))
content_str = json.dumps(content).lower()

# Check each field
gaps = []
if "5618487" not in content_str:
    gaps.append("RUC not in content")
```

### 3. Categorize Gaps

Use a structured gap analysis with emoji prefix:
- `🔴 Critical` — content is missing and page will 404 or error (e.g., privacidad/terminos content sections don't exist but pages reference them)
- `🟡 Medium` — content exists in questionnaire but not on site (testimonials, services, social links)
- `🟢 Minor` — nice-to-have info (business hours, city details)

### 4. Fix What Can Be Fixed NOW

**Testimonials:** Client may have provided 5+ real testimonials with permission. Replace the placeholder/generic ones. Include: name, country, service received, quote, rating. DO NOT fabricate names — use verified client names only.

**Social Links:** The client's questionnaire almost always has Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn, Wattpad, Amazon Author links that are nowhere on the site. Add them to the footer's `social` array and to the contact section.

**Missing Services:** The questionnaire may list 12+ services with prices, but the site may only show 8. Add the missing ones. Each needs: id, name, description, priceUSD, pricePYG, delivery time.

**Education/Credentials:** Client certifications (MIT, Google, etc.) and education rarely make it onto the about page. Add them even if the site already has a bio — this is high-CL (credibility load) content.

**RUC/Legal Name:** In Paraguay, displaying the RUC number on the footer is standard for professional services. Add it if missing.

**Work Process:** If the client provided a detailed work process (6+ steps with durations), add it to the services page as a visible process section.

**Blog:** If content has blog posts (`blog.posts`) but no `/blog` route exists, create the pages. Posts often have slug, title, excerpt, date, category, author, readingTime.

### 5. Sync to English (or secondary locale)

Most LATAM client sites are bilingual (ES/EN). After fixing ES content, sync the same changes to EN. Watch out for different key structures — ES may use `about.story.paragraphs` while EN uses `sobre.bio.features`. Check keys before blind-copying.

### 6. Generate/Save source-of-truth.md

If the client's filled questionnaire data is spread across conversation context and not saved to the repo, write it as `docs/source-of-truth.md`. This file becomes THE canonical reference for all future work. Structure it as:

```markdown
# Client Name — Source of Truth

> Complete client questionnaire answers. Canonical reference for all content.
> Source: Client onboarding session — Month Year

## PART A — CONFIRMATIONS

### A.1 Brand Identity
| Field | Confirmed Value | Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Business name | ✅ | ✅ |
| RUC | [REDACTED_PHONE] | ❌ NOT IN CONTENT |

### A.3 Services with Prices ✅
| # | Service | USD | PYG | Delivery | Implemented |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Custom Cover eBook | $45 | ₲300,000 | 1-2 weeks | ✅ |
```

### 7. What to Leave for Client Action

Some things cannot be fixed by the agent and must be surfaced as "Client Action Still Needed":
- **Image files** (premade catalog, professional photos, portfolio samples) — client must upload to Drive
- **Final brand palette** (exact hex codes, typography) — pending client design decisions
- **Mercado Pago/PayPal confirmation** — client must verify account
- **Domain registration** — client must purchase and point DNS

## Reference Implementation

**Full Dayah LitWorks audit documented in session:**
- Questionaire ingested: 20KB+ with 12 sections
- 10 gaps found and fixed
- `docs/source-of-truth.md` generated (14KB)
- `docs/client-profile.md` — client achievement audit
- All changes synced ES/EN and pushed

## Pitfalls

- **Git disagreements** — If the repo has diverged branches or force-push history, `git reset --hard origin/main` may wipe your changes. Always `git pull --rebase` first, or `git stash` before resetting.
- **EN vs ES key structures differ** — Never assume keys match. `content_es` might have `about` while `content_en` has `sobre`. Check both before syncing.
- **Content size limits** — `content/es.json` can reach 50KB+ with blog posts and full FAQ. Keep the JSON valid with `json.dump(ensure_ascii=False)` for Spanish characters.
- **Client provided testimonials but no explicit permission** — The questionnaire usually states permission status. Trust data from filled questionnaires. If unsure, use "Verified Client" instead of fabricated names.

Key lessons for Nico — the sites that look good have:

2. **Real images** — Oz has artwork, Dayah has book covers, Nexa has photos
3. **Consistent button sizing** (h-10 to h-12, px-8 to px-10)
4. **Section spacing** with py-16/px-6
5. **Sticky glass nav** with border-b
6. **Multiple CTA buttons** with shadow
7. Backgrounds are clean, not heavy gradients

Now I know exactly what to improve. Key learnings from the 3 best sites:

**Nexa** — clean white bg + gold accent + sticky glass nav (`bg-white/95 backdrop-blur-sm border-b`) + h-12 CTAs with shadow + section padding py-16/20
**Dayah** — purple/gold + `rounded-2xl bg-white/10 backdrop-blur-md p-8 sm:p-12` cards + responsive grids + solid separator sections
**Oz** — Inter + Playfair fonts, amber accents, `px-4 py-2 text-sm rounded-lg hover:` nav items

Nico's problems vs these:
1. All-dark, no light/white section — makes it feel one-note
2. `rounded-full` buttons instead of `rounded-lg` — less modern
3. No sticky nav pattern — we have `fixed` but no `border-b`
4. Card padding too tight (p-5 vs p-8 on Dayah)
5. No real images at all (0 images vs Oz/Dayah having art/covers)
6. No button shadows
7. No proper section divider styling

# Nexa Paraguay — AI Agent Guide

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://nexa.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/nexa-paraguay
- **Docker service:** nexa_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** Host(`nexa.paragu-ai.com`)

## Architecture
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → nexa_web:3000

## Pages
~15 pages: Home, Services (residency, tax, banking, company formation), Blog (guides), About, Contact. 4 languages: ES, EN, DE, NL.

## Design System
Professional blue/white. Clean, trustworthy, European-facing. Font: Inter.

## Content
Content lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales when applicable).
All text, services, products, FAQ items are editable there.

## Build & Deploy
```bash
npm run build
docker build -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml nexa
```

## Critical Patterns
- 4-language content (es/en/de/nl)
- Content-rich guides and blog posts
- Lead capture for European prospects
- WhatsApp + email CTAs

## Client Onboarding
See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.
See `docs/brand-guide.md` for brand identity details.

# Dayah LitWorks — AI Agent Guide

This is a **standalone Next.js 15 app** at **https://dayah.paragu-ai.com**.
Book cover design studio (Daihana Araujo, Asunción, est. 2019).

## Quick Links

- **Live site:** https://dayah.paragu-ai.com
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks
- **Docker service:** `dayah-litworks_web` (Docker Swarm, port 3000)
- **Traefik labels:** Host(`dayah.paragu-ai.com`), auto SSL via LetsEncrypt
- **VPS IP:** 72.61.44.159 (same as paragu-ai-builder, different Docker service)
- **Old PAB path:** `/s/es/dayah-litworks` → 308 redirects to `/`

## Architecture

```
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL, CDN)
  └── dayah.paragu-ai.com A → 72.61.44.159 (proxied)
        └── Traefik (Docker Swarm, port 443 → 3000)
              └── dayah-litworks_web (Next.js standalone, port 3000)
```

**Independent from paragu-ai-builder.** Changes here NEVER affect other clients.

## Repository Structure

```
dayah-litworks/
├── app/                          # Next.js App Router pages
│   ├── page.tsx                  # Home — hero, stats, services, portfolio, testimonials, process, CTA
│   ├── servicios/page.tsx        # Services + FAQ
│   ├── catalogo/page.tsx         # Portfolio with genre filter
│   ├── sobre/page.tsx            # About Daihana
│   ├── faq/page.tsx              # All FAQ items (accordion)
│   ├── contacto/page.tsx         # Contact cards (email, WhatsApp, Instagram)
│   ├── blog/page.tsx             # Blog index (5 posts)
│   ├── blog/[slug]/page.tsx      # Individual blog post
│   ├── privacidad/page.tsx       # Privacy policy
│   ├── terminos/page.tsx         # Terms with Daihana's 7 conditions
│   ├── not-found.tsx             # Custom 404 page
│   ├── layout.tsx                # Root layout (fonts, metadata, OG image)
│   ├── globals.css               # Tailwind v4 + @theme with brand colors
│   └── favicon.ico               # SVG favicon
├── components/                   # Section components (all standalone)
│   ├── header.tsx                # Sticky header with nav + locale toggle
│   ├── hero-section.tsx          # NOT USED — hero is inline in app/page.tsx
│   ├── stats.tsx                 # Stats counter grid
│   ├── services-section.tsx      # Services with dual-currency (USD+PYG), grouped by category
│   ├── portfolio-filtered.tsx    # Portfolio grid with genre filter buttons
│   ├── testimonials.tsx          # Testimonial cards 2x2 grid
│   ├── process.tsx               # Timeline with gradient connector
│   ├── faq.tsx                   # OLD — not imported anymore
│   ├── faq-section.tsx           # FAQ accordion (used)
│   ├── cta-banner.tsx            # Gradient CTA banner
│   ├── footer.tsx                # Dark footer with gradient border
│   ├── blog-card.tsx             # Blog post card
│   └── whatsapp-float.tsx        # Floating WhatsApp button (green, fixed position)
│   └── ui/
│       ├── button.tsx            # shadcn-based (CVA variants)
│       ├── badge.tsx             # shadcn-based
│       └── card.tsx              # shadcn-based
├── content/                      # ALL content (no database)
│   ├── es.json                   # Spanish content (62KB, 564 data points)
│   ├── en.json                   # English translation (59KB)
│   ├── tokens.json               # Design tokens (colors, components)
│   └── blog/                     # 5 markdown blog posts
│       ├── guia-de-tipografia-para-autores.md
│       ├── [REDACTED_TOKEN].md
│       ├── [REDACTED_TOKEN].md
│       ├── marketing-para-autores-independientes.md
│       └── [REDACTED_TOKEN].md
├── lib/
│   ├── engine.ts                 # Content resolution (dot-path getter + placeholder fill)
│   └── utils.ts                  # cn() utility (clsx + tailwind-merge)
├── public/
│   └── images/covers/            # 32 images (11 real JPGs + 13 SVGs + 8 misc)
├── next.config.ts                # output: standalone, redirects /s/* → /
├── Dockerfile                    # Multi-stage Next.js standalone build
├── docker-compose.yml            # Swarm stack with Traefik labels
└── package.json                  # Deps: next, react, lucide-react, clsx, framer-motion
```

## Content Architecture

All content is in `content/es.json`. No database, no external API. Structure:

```json
{
  "home": {
    "hero": { "headline": "...", "subheadline": "...", "ctaPrimaryText": "...", "backgroundImage": "/images/...", "overlayColor": "rgba(10,10,20,0.88)" },
    "stats": { "items": [{ "value": "+80", "label": "Proyectos entregados" }] },
    "services": { "title": "Nuestros Servicios", "items": [{ "name": "...", "priceUSD": "$45", "pricePYG": "₲300.000", "delivery": "1–2 semanas", "includes": ["..."] }] },
    "portfolio": { "title": "Trabajos Recientes", "items": [{ "title": "...", "imageUrl": "/images/...", "category": "Romance" }] },
    "testimonials": { "title": "Lo que dicen mis clientes", "items": [{ "text": "...", "author": "María González", "rating": 5 }] },
    "process": { "title": "Proceso", "steps": [{ "number": 1, "title": "...", "description": "..." }] },
    "faq": { "items": [{ "q": "¿Cuánto tarda?", "a": "..." }] }
  },
  "ctaBanner": { "title": "¿Listo para tu próxima portada?", "buttonText": "Quiero mi portada", "buttonHref": "https://wa.me/[REDACTED_PHONE]" },
  "footer": { "email": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]" }
}
```

## Design System (Tailwind CSS v4 @theme)

Defined in `app/globals.css`:

```css
@theme {
  --color-primary: #94abd6;
  --color-secondary: #d43d5e;
  --color-background: #0a0a14;
  --color-surface: #11132a;
  --color-surface-light: #1b2040;
  --color-foreground: #eaeaea;
  --color-muted-foreground: #a0aac8;
  --color-border: #1b2040;
}
```

Fonts: Playfair Display (headings, 800 weight) + Inter (body, 400 weight).

## Image Assets

| Category | Count | Type | Resolution |
|----------|-------|------|------------|
| Real cover JPGs | 11 | Client's actual work | 600×900 (book ratio) |
| Gradient premade SVGs | 6 | Real cover art with brand gradient | SVG vector |
| Gradient placeholder SVGs | 8 | "COVER / Dayah LitWorks" fallback | SVG vector |
| Service card SVGs | 4 | Service illustrations (unused, for future) | SVG vector |
| Brand assets | 4 | logo-blanco.svg, hero-bg.svg, dlw-master.png, og-dayah.png | — |

Real covers are referenced in BOTH `home.portfolio.items[].imageUrl` (home page) AND `home.products.items[].imageUrl` (catalog page). SVG placeholders are only in `home.products.items[].image` (catalog fallback).

## Services (8 items, 3 categories)

- **Portadas Personalizadas** (3): eBook $45/₲300K, Paperback $80/₲500K, Combo $120/₲800K
- **Portadas Premade** (2): eBook $35/₲250K, Combo $80/₲500K
- **Maquetación Interior** (3): eBook $25/₲160K, Paperback $35/₲250K, Combo $50/₲320K

- **Live:** https://nexa.paragu-ai.com

- **Docker service:** nexa_web (2 replicas)

Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → nexa_web:3000

## Pages
~15 pages: Home, Services (residency, tax, banking, company formation), Blog (guides), About, Contact. 4 languages: ES, EN, DE, NL.

Professional blue/white. Clean, trustworthy, European-facing. Font: Inter.

Content lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales when applicable).
All text, services, products, FAQ items are editable there.

```bash
npm run build
docker build -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml nexa
```

- WhatsApp + email CTAs

See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.

# Dayah LitWorks — AI Agent Guide

This is a **standalone Next.js 15 app** at **https://dayah.paragu-ai.com**.
Book cover design studio (Daihana Araujo, Asunción, est. 2019).

## Quick Links

- **Live site:** https://dayah.paragu-ai.com
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks
- **Docker service:** `dayah-litworks_web` (Docker Swarm, port 3000)
- **Traefik labels:** Host(`dayah.paragu-ai.com`), auto SSL via LetsEncrypt
- **VPS IP:** 72.61.44.159 (same as paragu-ai-builder, different Docker service)
- **Old PAB path:** `/s/es/dayah-litworks` → 308 redirects to `/`

## Architecture

```
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL, CDN)

        └── Traefik (Docker Swarm, port 443 → 3000)
              └── dayah-litworks_web (Next.js standalone, port 3000)
```

**Independent from paragu-ai-builder.** Changes here NEVER affect other clients.

**What was analyzed from other repos and applied:**

**Dayah pattern** (dayah-litworks):
- `rounded-2xl` cards with `backdrop-blur` + subtle border — replaces clunky `glass-card` class
- `px-5 py-4` vs p-4 (better padding ratio)
- `flex-1` on card descriptions keeps cards equal height
- `rounded-xl` on nested elements (icon area, tag pills)

**Oz Montania pattern** (ozmontania-website):
- `sticky top-0 border-b border-zinc-800/50 z-50 backdrop-blur-md` — modern glass nav
- `rounded-lg px-3 py-2 text-sm font-medium hover:bg-surface` — nav item pattern
- `section-title` with `font-serif` + `clamp()` sizing
- `animate-fade-in-up` → renamed to `animate-in` for entry animations
- Timeline dots with `shadow-lg shadow-mystic-500/30`

**Nexa pattern** (nexa-paraguay):
- `h-11` CTAs with `rounded-lg` and `shadow-lg` — replaces `rounded-full`
- `bg-white/95 backdrop-blur-sm` sticky header → adapted as `rgba(15,10,26,0.92)`
- `py-20 md:py-28` section spacing
- `border-t border-mystic-800/20` as section divider instead of heavy gradients
- Clean, minimal hero without 3 orbs (reduced to 2 subtle ones)

**Typography upgrade:**
- Google Fonts loaded: Playfair Display (headings, --font-heading) + Inter (body, --font-body)
- Same stack as Oz and Nexa — premium professional pairing

{"output": "===\nNicolás Duarte — HR IT Analyst · People Analytics · Data Systems · AI | Nicolás Duarte

ExperienceSkillsPortfolioRecommendationsContact

Hi, I'm

Nicolás Duarte

HR IT Analyst

HR · Data · AI — Hybrid profile at the intersection of enterprise HR systems, data analytics, and AI workflows.

I work in global HR IT at Nestlé — I keep enterprise HR systems running across countries, investigate failures, and build the tools that make operations more efficient. One of two analysts globally trusted with mass payroll data loads for entire country workforces. Created an HR IT Audit service from zero that reduced mislabeled errors by 95% across LATAM.

View PortfolioGet in TouchDownload CV
EXPERIENCE

Experience

People Analytics & Data Strategy

Intersection of HR, IT, and Data

Jun 2023 – Present · 3 yrs

HR IT Analyst (Global) · Nestlé

  • Mass payroll data loads — Brazil (~22,000 employees), Mexico (~10,000), zero errors, zero rework. Youngest analyst globally trusted with this scope.
  • HR IT Audit Service — created from zero. 500+ audits completed across LATAM. Reduced mislabeled errors by 95%+. Leadership acknowledged as team deliverable.
  • Forensic incident resolution — primary diagnostic authority for high-stakes SAP SuccessFactors incidents. 75+ tickets/week, well below 24h closure target.
  • Service quality reporting — created from zero. KPIs and analysis framework now used by leadership team in their own decisions.
  • Replaced external consultants as the bridge between business clients and Global IT for requirements translation.
  • Dashboard architecture — building unified HR IT Analytics Dashboard (6 data sources, sole owner, officially scoped in Nestlé 2026 team goals).
  • AI-automated audit workflow — integrating Microsoft Copilot and Power Automate into audit processes (Q2 2026).
  • Global scope — 24/7 follow-the-sun model covering all timezones. Top global performer among peers.

Reporting Analyst

People Analytics · LATAM

Jun 2023 – Jun 2024 · 1 yr

Reporting Analyst (Shared Resource) · Nestlé

  • Built 10+ reports from scratch for HR leaders across 10+ LATAM countries — comp analysis, compensation design, headcount costs, workforce distribution.
  • Owned compensation analytics — salary band analysis, comp benchmarking, headcount cost breakdowns across markets.
  • SAP HR & SuccessFactors administration, cross-market support, and system testing.

Data Analyst

Central Consultant Inc.

May 2021 – May 2023 · 2 yrs

Data Analyst · Central Consultant Inc.

  • Complex data analysis including data quality frameworks, error correction, and accuracy improvement.
  • 100% design and execution of road signage design project. Prepared 15+ audit reports for senior management.

General Operations Coordinator

AIESEC en Paraguay

Feb 2022 – Aug 2023 · 1.5 yrs

Coordinator · AIESEC en Paraguay

  • Designed and executed local strategy achieving 70% increase in metric achievement.
  • Volunteer coordination, recruitment, psychometric assessment — 8.5/10 satisfaction rating.

Look at the last part of the CSS — the `:root` block at the very bottom still has **old navy values**:

```
--primary:#1e3a5f;
--accent:#c9753e;
```

The CSS variables in the `:root` block override the custom properties from the `@theme` inline. The build pipelined the old values from `globals.css` into the compiled CSS. The build didn't pick up the new values because Next.js 16 inlines CSS variables at build time from the source `:root` block.

Wait — the build DID run with the new values. But the OLD CSS is being served because CF is caching. The new CSS file has a different name (`0sl_09ducdeuc.css`) vs whatever the old one was. But the `:root` values in the output at the bottom still show old navy because this is CACHED CSS from CF.

The new deployed container has a different CSS hash. The browser is still receiving old cached content. CF needs time to propagate or needs a manual purge.

Let me verify what's in the container:

messages.content: {"output": "===", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}
The CSS is served differently by Next.js 16's Turbopack — the CSS variables are in the inline `:root` that gets compiled. Let me check the built output directly:

# Paragu-AI Builder - AI Agent Configuration

> AI-first development configuration for the Universal Website Generation Engine
> This file guides AI agents working on this project

## Project Context

**Paragu-AI Builder** is a multi-tenant, multi-locale website generation engine for Paraguayan businesses. It uses:
- **Next.js 15** + **TypeScript** + **Tailwind 3.4.19**
- **Supabase** (PostgreSQL + Auth)
- **Cloudflare Pages** (deployment)
- **Token-based theming** (CSS variables, not hardcoded colors)
- **21 reusable section components** serving 20+ business types

## AI Agent Quick Start

### When Starting Work on This Project

1. **Read This File** - You are here! ✅
2. **Read CLAUDE.md** - Technical architecture and coding standards
3. **Check Current State** - Run `git status` to see what's happening
4. **Check Open PRs** - Run `gh pr list` to see in-flight work
5. **Review Recent Commits** - Run `git log --oneline -10`

### Critical Rules (NEVER Break These)

```
⚠️  DO NOT upgrade Tailwind to v4 (breaks build)
⚠️  DO NOT hardcode colors - always use var(--primary)
⚠️  DO NOT skip business_id in database queries
⚠️  DO NOT commit .env files
⚠️  DO NOT silently catch errors - always log/rethrow
```

## Project Structure for AI Agents

```
src/                         # Data layer (JSON configs)
  ├── schemas/               # Business type schemas
  ├── tokens/               # Design tokens (colors, fonts)
  ├── registry/             # Business type registry
  └── content/              # Content templates

web/                        # Application layer
  ├── app/
  │   ├── [business]/       # Generated sites (SSG)
  │   ├── admin/            # Admin dashboard
  │   ├── api/              # API routes
  │   └── s/[locale]/       # Multi-tenant sites
  ├── components/
  │   ├── sections/         # 21 reusable sections
  │   └── ui/              # UI primitives (CVA-based)
  ├── lib/
  │   ├── supabase/         # Database clients
  │   ├── engine/           # Template engine
  │   └── types.ts          # TypeScript types
  └── scripts/              # Build/generation scripts
```

## AI-Specific Patterns

### 1. Adding New Business Type (Most Common Task)

When adding a new business type (e.g., `dentist`, `mechanic`):

**Required Files:**
```
src/
├── registry/[type].type.json          # Section config, features
├── tokens/[type].tokens.json         # Colors, fonts
├── content/[type].content.json       # Spanish copy templates
└── schemas/[type].schema.json        # Validation schema
```

**Reuses:**
- ✅ Existing section components from `web/components/sections/`
- ✅ Base tokens from `src/tokens/base.tokens.json`
- ✅ No new React components needed (usually)

**Example (5-minute add):**
```json
// src/registry/dentist.type.json
{
  "id": "dentist",
  "nameEs": "Consultorio Dental",
  "tokens": "dentist",
  "sections": ["hero", "services", "team", "testimonials", "contact"],
  "features": { "booking": true, "emergency": true }
}
```

### 2. Adding New Section Component

When adding a new reusable section:

**File:** `web/components/sections/[name]-section.tsx`

**Pattern:**
```typescript
interface [Name]SectionProps {
  title: string
  items: Array<{...}>
  // Theme automatically applied via CSS variables
}

export function [Name]Section({ title, items }: [Name]SectionProps) {
  return (
    <section className="py-16 bg-[var(--background)]">
      <h2 className="text-[var(--primary)]">{title}</h2>
      {/* Content */}
    </section>
  )
}
```

**Registration:** Add to `web/lib/engine/renderer.tsx`

### 3. Database Schema Changes

When modifying Supabase schema:

**Location:** `supabase/migrations/000_complete_schema.sql`

**Rules:**
- Add new tables at the end
- Include RLS policies for every table
- Add indexes for query performance
- Create views for analytics if needed
- Test with `npm run typecheck`

### 4. Environment Variables

**Local:** `web/.env.local` (gitignored)
**Production:** Cloudflare Pages dashboard

**Required:**
```
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=
SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=
```

**Optional:**
```
MERCADOPAGO_PUBLIC_KEY=
MERCADOPAGO_ACCESS_TOKEN=
NEXT_PUBLIC_GA_MEASUREMENT_ID=
```

## AI-Optimized Workflows

### Workflow 1: Generate Preview Site for Lead

**Context:** Admin wants to generate a demo site for a lead

**Steps:**
1. Query lead from `leads` table
2. Compose page using `composePage()` from business type
3. Generate static HTML
4. Store in `generated_sites` table
5. Return preview URL

**Key Files:**
- `web/lib/engine/compose.ts` - Page composition
- `web/lib/engine/renderer.tsx` - Section rendering
- `web/app/admin/leads/page.tsx` - Admin UI

### Workflow 2: WhatsApp Outreach

**Context:** Send templated WhatsApp message to lead

**Steps:**
1. Get lead phone/whatsapp from `leads` table
2. Generate wa.me link with templated message
3. Log event to `outreach_events` table
4. Update lead status to `contacted`

**Template Location:** `web/lib/outreach/templates.ts` (create if needed)

### Workflow 3: MercadoPago Payment

**Context:** Lead wants to subscribe

**Steps:**
1. Create subscription in `subscriptions` table
2. Generate MercadoPago checkout
3. Handle webhook at `/api/webhooks/mercadopago`
4. Update subscription status on payment
5. Grant access to self-service editing

## Common AI Tasks & Solutions

### Task: Fix Build Error

**Checklist:**
1. `npm run typecheck` - TypeScript errors
2. `npm run lint` - ESLint errors
3. Check for Tailwind v4 (should be 3.4.19)
4. Check for hardcoded colors (should use CSS vars)
5. Check for missing `business_id` in queries

### Task: Debug Database Connection

**Checklist:**
1. `.env.local` exists with correct keys
2. Supabase project is running
3. RLS policies allow the operation
4. Using correct client (server vs browser vs admin)

### Task: Add New Feature

**Questions to Ask:**
1. Can this reuse existing sections? (95% of cases: YES)
2. Does it need new database tables? (check schema first)
3. Is it admin-only or public? (affects auth)
4. Does it need MercadoPago? (payments feature)

### Task: Optimize Performance

**Strategies:**
1. Use Server Components by default
2. Lazy load heavy components
3. Add database indexes for common queries
4. Use Cloudflare caching rules
5. Minimize client-side JavaScript

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

# Professional Portfolio Site

Scaffold a production-ready single-page portfolio using Next.js 15 + Tailwind CSS 4 with a custom color theme. Includes a structured "roast → fix → redeploy" loop for when the user says it looks bad.

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Gather Profile Data

Confirm: name, profession/title, employer+role (past+present), key projects (3-6 with title/desc/tech/metrics), skills (8-12), contact channels, color preference. Extract from prior session context when available.

### Phase 2: Scaffold Project

```bash
mkdir -p /tmp/{name}-portfolio && cd $_
git init
```

**Always create .gitignore BEFORE first `git add`:**
```
node_modules/
.next/
out/
*.tsbuildinfo
next-env.d.ts
```

**Always include in source:**
- `package.json` + `next.config.js` with `output: 'export'`
- `tsconfig.json`
- `app/layout.tsx` with OpenGraph + metadata + icons
- `app/globals.css` with `@theme` block
- `app/page.tsx` — single-page layout
- `public/favicon.svg`, `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`

### Phase 3: Theme System

Define colors in `globals.css` using CSS variables + `@theme` block. See `references/mystic-purple-theme.md` for the reference palette.

**CRITICAL — Color unification rule:** Every color value on the page must come from the same named palette. Never mix palette names (no `from-purple-` + `from-mystic-` + `from-violet-`). After writing the initial theme, scan with:
```
grep -n 'from-purple\|from-violet\|from-fuchsia\|from-indigo\|from-pink\|rgba(139,' app/page.tsx app/globals.css
```
Replace any stragglers.

### Phase 4: Page Structure

Single-page layout with these sections:

1. **Header** — `fixed top-0 left-0 right-0 z-50 border-b border-{color}-800/30 backdrop-blur-md` with nav links
2. **Hero** — full `min-h-screen`, animated background orbs (keep to 2 max), stats bar, two CTAs, scroll indicator
3. **Projects** — 3-column card grid, each with decorative gradient banner, title, summary, tags
4. **Skills** — 2-column animated skill bars with gradient fill
5. **Experience** — timeline with dots, shadow glow on active role
6. **Contact** — centered card with contact channel icons
7. **Footer** — copyright line

### Phase 5: Typography

Use a premium font stack from Phase 7 learnings:

```css
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@300;400;500;600;700;800&family=Playfair+Display:wght@400;500;600;700;800&display=swap');

@theme {
  --font-heading: "Playfair Display", serif;
  --font-body: "Inter", sans-serif;
}
```

Apply: `style={{fontFamily:'var(--font-heading)'}}` on H1, H2, section title, stat numbers. Body inherits Inter via body rule.

### Phase 6: Visual Audit (critical — do this before deploy)

Before any deploy, run a browser-based audit. Navigate to the live site and check:

```javascript
// Card heights
const cards = document.querySelectorAll('.glass-card, [class*="rounded-"]');
[...cards].map(c => ({h: c.offsetHeight, text: c.textContent.substring(0,30)}));

// Page dimensions
JSON.stringify({
  heroH: document.querySelector('section').offsetHeight,
  totalH: document.body.scrollHeight,
  viewportH: window.innerHeight,
  screens: (document.body.scrollHeight / window.innerHeight).toFixed(1),
  images: document.querySelectorAll('img').length,
  totalHeight: document.body.scrollHeight
});
```

**Quality gates:**
- Card heights: target ~300-450px each. If cards are 1200+px, an inner element has no explicit height. Fix: set `height: 112px !important` or `h-28` on mockup/dash containers.
- Screens total: aim for 8-12 viewport screens. If >20, compress text, reduce card sizes.
- Hero: must naturally fill viewport (`min-h-screen`). If hero is 312px tall with 23 screens of page, it's too small.
- Images: 0 images = warning. Use decorative SVG icons as placeholder.
- No horizontal overflow: `document.documentElement.scrollWidth > window.innerWidth` must be false.

### Phase 7: Design Upgrade Patterns (applied from competitor analysis)

When user says "it looks ugly", apply these upgrades in order:

**A. Typography upgrade (highest impact per line of code)**
- Swap system fonts for Inter (body) + Playfair Display (headings) via Google Fonts
- Use `--font-heading` on stat numbers, section titles, H1/H2
- Set `-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased` on body

**B. Nav upgrade (Nexa pattern)**
```
sticky top-0 z-50 border-b border-{color}-800/30 backdrop-blur-md
nav links: rounded-lg px-3 py-2 text-sm font-medium hover:bg-{color}-900/40 transition-all
```

**C. Card upgrade (Dayah pattern)**
- Replace glass-card class with inline `rounded-2xl` with inline style `background: rgba(...)` + `backdropFilter: 'blur(8px)'` + `border: 1px solid rgba(...)`
- Use `flex-1` on descriptions so cards equal-height
- Add `hover:-translate-y-1 transition-all`
- Reduce padding from `p-6` to `px-5 py-4`

**D. CTA button upgrade (Nexa pattern)**
- Replace `rounded-full` CTAs with `rounded-lg`
- Use `inline-flex h-11 items-center justify-center rounded-lg px-8 text-sm font-semibold`
- Add `shadow-lg shadow-{color}-600/25` on primary CTA

**E. Section spacing (Nexa pattern)**
- Consistent `py-20 md:py-28` on all sections
- Use `border-t border-{color}-800/20` as section dividers instead of heavy gradient separators

**F. Entry animations (Oz pattern)**
- Add `@keyframes fadeInUp` animation in CSS
- `animate-in` class for stats/cards appearing on load
- Subtle floating animation on hero background orbs (`float` keyframes)

**G. Experience timeline polish**
- Add `shadow-lg shadow-{color}-500/30` on the active job's timeline dot
- Timeline line: `bg-gradient-to-b from-{color}-500/40 via-{color}-500/10 to-transparent`
- Passive roles at `opacity-60`

### Phase 8: Git & Deploy

```bash
cd /tmp/{name}-portfolio
git add -A && git commit -m "..." && git push

# Build export
npm install && npx next build

# Docker
docker build -t {name}-portfolio:latest .
docker stack deploy -c - {name}-portfolio << 'STACK'
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    image: {name}-portfolio:latest
    deploy:
      labels:
        - "traefik.enable=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.rule=Host(`{subdomain}.paragu-ai.com`)"
        - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.entrypoints=websecure"
        - "traefik.http.routers.{name}-portfolio.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
        - "traefik.http.services.{name}-portfolio.loadbalancer.server.port=80"
        - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
    networks:
      - agent-net
networks:
  agent-net:
    external: true
STACK

# Verify
curl -sI https://{subdomain}.paragu-ai.com
```

### Phase 9: Hot-Reload Redesign Loop

When user says "ugly" after deploy:
1. Edit `app/page.tsx` and `app/globals.css` locally
2. `npx next build`
3. `docker build -t {name}-portfolio:latest .`
4. `docker service update --image {name}-portfolio:latest {name}-portfolio_web`
5. `docker run --rm --network agent-net nginx:alpine curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://{name}-portfolio_web/`
6. `git add -A && git commit && git push`

If docker service update doesn't pick up new image (same digest), add a no-op layer to Dockerfile:
```dockerfile
RUN echo $(date +%s) > /build-id
```

### Phase 10: Competitor Design Benchmarking

When the user says "looks bad compared to other sites", benchmark against:

1. **Oz Montania** (ozmontania.paragu-ai.com) — dark theme, amber accents, Inter+Playfair fonts, glass nav, `rounded-lg px-3 py-2` nav items, `animate-fade-in-up`, `btn-primary`/`btn-outline` classes, `container-art` with responsive padding
2. **Dayah LitWorks** (dayah.paragu-ai.com) — dark theme, purple+gold, `rounded-2xl bg-white/10 backdrop-blur-md p-8 sm:p-12` cards, content-driven sections, 32 images, responsive grid layouts
3. **Nexa Paraguay** (nexa.paragu-ai.com) — light theme, navy+gold, `sticky top-0 z-50 border-b border-border bg-white/95 backdrop-blur-sm`, `h-12` CTAs with `rounded-lg` + shadow, `py-20/16/24` section spacing

Extract the specific CSS class patterns (shown above) and apply to Nico's portfolio. Don't copy the color — copy the spacing, sizing, and layout patterns.

## Pitfalls

- **node_modules committed** — If `npm install` ran before .gitignore existed: `git rm -r --cached node_modules .next out && git filter-branch --force --index-filter 'git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch -r node_modules .next out' --prune-empty --tag-name-filter cat -- --all && git push --force`
- **Card blowout** — A `dash-frame` without explicit `height` can stretch to 1278px. Always set `height: 112px !important` or use `h-28` with `!important`.
- **Color leak** — One uncought `from-purple-500` or `rgba(139,92,246,` breaks the unified theme. Use `grep -n` check.
- **Zero images** — A portfolio with 0 real images looks unfinished. Add decorative SVG icons as placeholder. Flag the user needs to add screenshots.
- **DNS via Cloudflare proxied** — `*.paragu-ai.com` uses CF proxied DNS. Traefik's HTTP-01 challenge works through proxy (CF forwards port 80). Just ensure the subdomain resolves to VPS IP.
- **Static export** — Always use `output: 'export'` for nginx deployment. No server runtime needed.
- **Don't rebuild docker if digest unchanged** — Swarm won't redeploy. Use `RUN echo $(date +%s) > /build-id` as no-op layer to force new digest.

A dark, ethereal, cosmic purple palette for portfolio sites. Deep near-black background with vibrant purple accents.

```css

```

{"action": "patch", "name": "professional-portfolio", "new_string": "### Phase 7: Design Upgrade Patterns (applied from competitor analysis)

When user says "it looks bad" or "compare with [other site]", benchmark against premium sites by extracting their CSS patterns systematically.

**Competitor CSS extraction method:**
1. Navigate to the target site: `browser_navigate(url)`
2. Extract nav pattern: `browser_console('getComputedStyle(document.querySelector("header, nav")).background')`
3. Extract card pattern: `browser_console('[document.querySelectorAll("section > div > div")].slice(0,5).map(d => ({cls: d.className, bg: getComputedStyle(d).background, h: d.offsetHeight}))')`  
4. Extract CTA pattern: look for primary buttons, check `background`, `padding`, `borderRadius`, `boxShadow`, `fontSize`
5. Extract body: `getComputedStyle(document.body).fontFamily, .background`
6. Extract section spacing: `[...document.querySelectorAll("section")].slice(0,3).map(s => ({cls: getComputedStyle(s).padding}))`", "old_string": "### Phase 7: Design Upgrade Patterns (applied from competitor analysis)"}

{"action": "patch", "name": "professional-portfolio", "new_string": "- **Don't rebuild docker if digest unchanged** — Swarm won't redeploy. Use `RUN echo $(date +%s) > /build-id` as no-op layer to force new digest.
- **Docker service collision** — Two Docker Swarm services with Traefik labels for the SAME hostname will compete. Traefik routes to whichever one it picks first (non-deterministic). Always check existing services: `docker service ls --filter name={name}`, and remove the old one: `docker service rm {name}_web` before deploying a replacement.
- **Cloudflare cache stale** — After Docker deploy, CF may serve cached old CSS/JS for minutes. The container itself has the new code (verify with `docker run --rm {image} grep "newcolor" /app/.next/static/chunks/*.css`), but browsers see cached version. Hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) or wait for cache TTL.
- **Next.js 16 vs 15** — If the existing repo uses Next.js 16 (detect by `"next": "^16"` in package.json or Turbopack chunk names like `0a79n_mzfgy10.css`): builds differently, uses Turbopack, CSS is inlined in JS chunks, `.next/` structure different. Don't assume `output: 'export'` works. Check for `next.config.ts` with server runtime config.
- **Real content > placeholder** — A portfolio with generic "Power BI dashboards" copy that doesn't match the actual person's real projects feels fake. If the repo already has real content (i18n JSON files with specific projects, metrics, quotes), use THAT repo instead of creating a new one. Check `IvanWeissVanDerPol/{name}-site` or `IvanWeissVanDerPol/{name}-career` repos before creating from scratch.", "old_string": "- **Don't rebuild docker if digest unchanged** — Swarm won't redeploy.
